clusters.title: Iran vows to destroy Middle East water and energy facilities if US attacks power plants
clusters.neutral_headline: Iranian missiles injure 180 in towns near Israeli nuclear site
bullet_summary: Striking Iran’s energy sites, including the Bushehr nuclear power plant, could violate the Geneva Conventions, FRANCE 24’s Douglas Herbert said in an analysis
Among the injured in the attacks on Arad and Dimona were a 12-year-old boy and a five-year-old girl, both reported to be in serious condition
The Israeli broadcaster Channel 13 reported early indications of possible deaths, though there was no official confirmation
Iranian missile strikes on two southern Israeli towns wounded more than 100 people on Saturday, medics said, after Israeli air defence systems failed to intercept the projectiles

items.title: Iran vows to destroy Middle East water and energy facilities if US attacks power plants
items.standfirst: Tehran’s response to Trump’s threat signals a potentially dangerous escalation as both sides menace sites relied on by millionsMiddle East crisis live – latest updatesTehran has said it will “irreversibly destroy” essential infrastructure across the Middle East, including vital water systems, if the US follows through on Donald Trump’s threat to “obliterate” Iran’s power plants unless the strait of Hormuz is fully opened within two days.As Iranian missiles struck two southern Israeli cities overnight, injuring dozens of people, and Tehran deployed long-range missiles for the first time, the developments signalled a dangerous potential escalation of the war, now in its fourth week, with both sides threatening facilities relied on by millions of people. Continue reading...
items.content: Tehran’s response to Trump’s threat signals a potentially dangerous escalation as both sides menace sites relied on by millionsMiddle East crisis live – latest updatesTehran has said it will “irreversibly destroy” essential infrastructure across the Middle East, including vital water systems, if the US follows through on Donald Trump’s threat to “obliterate” Iran’s power plants unless the strait of Hormuz is fully opened within two days.As Iranian missiles struck two southern Israeli cities overnight, injuring dozens of people, and Tehran deployed long-range missiles for the first time, the developments signalled a dangerous potential escalation of the war, now in its fourth week, with both sides threatening facilities relied on by millions of people. Continue reading...

items.title: Trump threat to hit Iran power plants could violate Geneva Conventions
items.standfirst: US President Donald Trump threatened overnight to “obliterate” Iran’s power plants if Tehran does not fully reopen the Strait of Hormuz within 48 hours, barely a day after suggesting he was “winding down” the war. Striking Iran’s energy sites, including the Bushehr nuclear power plant, could violate the Geneva Conventions, FRANCE 24’s Douglas Herbert said in an analysis.
items.content: US President Donald Trump threatened overnight to “obliterate” Iran’s power plants if Tehran does not fully reopen the Strait of Hormuz within 48 hours, barely a day after suggesting he was “winding down” the war. Striking Iran’s energy sites, including the Bushehr nuclear power plant, could violate the Geneva Conventions, FRANCE 24’s Douglas Herbert said in an analysis.

items.title: 'Disaster' for Gulf states if Iran targets desalination plants
items.standfirst: Iran on Saturday threatened to retaliate by targeting key infrastructure in neighbouring countries if US President Donald Trump follows through on his vow to “obliterate” the Islamic Republic’s power plants amid tensions over a blockage of the Strait of Hormuz. Journalist Hoda Abdel-Hamid, reporting from Doha, warned that such action would be a “disaster” for Gulf countries, noting that an attack on water desalination plants would be especially devastating as these nations depend on them for survival due to the lack of natural freshwater sources.
items.content: Iran on Saturday threatened to retaliate by targeting key infrastructure in neighbouring countries if US President Donald Trump follows through on his vow to “obliterate” the Islamic Republic’s power plants amid tensions over a blockage of the Strait of Hormuz. Journalist Hoda Abdel-Hamid, reporting from Doha, warned that such action would be a “disaster” for Gulf countries, noting that an attack on water desalination plants would be especially devastating as these nations depend on them for survival due to the lack of natural freshwater sources.

items.title: Iran threatens key infrastructure in Middle East after Trump ultimatum
items.standfirst: Iran threatened on Sunday to attack key infrastructure across the Middle East if US President Donald Trump follows through on his vow to "obliterate" the Islamic republic's power plants unless the Strait of Hormuz swiftly reopens. Trump ratcheted up pressure on Iran's leadership, announcing a countdown over the Islamic republic's de facto blockade on the crucial trade route.
items.content: Iran threatened on Sunday to attack key infrastructure across the Middle East if US President Donald Trump follows through on his vow to "obliterate" the Islamic republic's power plants unless the Strait of Hormuz swiftly reopens. Trump ratcheted up pressure on Iran's leadership, announcing a countdown over the Islamic republic's de facto blockade on the crucial trade route.

items.title: Iranian missile strikes injure more than 100 in southern Israel
items.standfirst: Iranian missile strikes on two southern Israeli towns wounded more than 100 people on Saturday, medics said, after Israeli air defence systems failed to intercept the projectiles. The two direct hits tore open the fronts of residential buildings and carved craters into the ground.

clusters.title: Cuba begins to restore electricity after nationwide power grid collapse
clusters.neutral_headline: BBC in Cuba as island plunged into darkness
bullet_summary: The blackout comes as Havana said it is open to “a serious and responsible dialogue” with the United States
Cuba, under a US blockade since 1962, has faced repeated fuel crises, said Helen Yaffe, professor of Latin American political economy at the University of Glasgow

items.title: Cuba begins to restore electricity after nationwide power grid collapse
items.standfirst: Cuba has begun restarting its power grid after another nationwide blackout left millions without electricity.
items.content: Cuba has begun restarting its power grid after another nationwide blackout left millions without electricity.

items.title: Cuba plunged into darkness amid nationwide blackout
items.standfirst: Cuban authorities scrambled Sunday to restore power to the island after the second nationwide blackout in less than a week, as the grid struggles due to an aging infrastructure and a US oil blockade. Some parts of Havana began to have power again, but others were still dark early Sunday, a day after the energy ministry reported a "total disconnection" of the national electric system in the country of nearly 10 million people.
items.content: Cuban authorities scrambled Sunday to restore power to the island after the second nationwide blackout in less than a week, as the grid struggles due to an aging infrastructure and a US oil blockade. Some parts of Havana began to have power again, but others were still dark early Sunday, a day after the energy ministry reported a "total disconnection" of the national electric system in the country of nearly 10 million people.

items.title: 'How long can any nation survive without oil?': Cuba faces repeated blackouts amid blockade
items.standfirst: Cuba was plunged into darkness for the second time in less than a week on Saturday after its national power grid failed again, strained by aging infrastructure and a US oil blockade. The blackout comes as Havana said it is open to “a serious and responsible dialogue” with the United States. Cuba, under a US blockade since 1962, has faced repeated fuel crises, said Helen Yaffe, professor of Latin American political economy at the University of Glasgow. She noted that renewable energy now helps meet daytime demand, with solar power covering nearly half of needs, but the country still lacks storage capacity and fuel for transport. "How long can any nation survive without oil?" she asked.
items.content: Cuba was plunged into darkness for the second time in less than a week on Saturday after its national power grid failed again, strained by aging infrastructure and a US oil blockade. The blackout comes as Havana said it is open to “a serious and responsible dialogue” with the United States. Cuba, under a US blockade since 1962, has faced repeated fuel crises, said Helen Yaffe, professor of Latin American political economy at the University of Glasgow. She noted that renewable energy now helps meet daytime demand, with solar power covering nearly half of needs, but the country still lacks storage capacity and fuel for transport. "How long can any nation survive without oil?" she asked.

items.title: Democratic Rep. Jason Crow says "what I'm not going to do is just throw money at the Iran war"
items.standfirst: Democratic Rep. Jason Crow of Colorado, an Afghan war veteran, told "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan" that he would not support an Iran war supplemental before Congress, but pushed back against the suggestion that he does not support the troops. "What I'm not going to do is just throw money at the Iran war," Crow added.
items.content: Democratic Rep. Jason Crow of Colorado, an Afghan war veteran, told "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan" that he would not support an Iran war supplemental before Congress, but pushed back against the suggestion that he does not support the troops. "What I'm not going to do is just throw money at the Iran war," Crow added.

items.title: IAEA director says no war would destroy Iran's nuclear ambitions, "unless it was nuclear war"
items.standfirst: Rafael Grossi, the International Atomic Energy Agency director, told "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan" that he doesn't think "any war" would destroy Iran's nuclear ambitions "unless it was nuclear war, and you go for destruction in an unfathomable way, which we hope, of course, will never be the case."
items.content: Rafael Grossi, the International Atomic Energy Agency director, told "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan" that he doesn't think "any war" would destroy Iran's nuclear ambitions "unless it was nuclear war, and you go for destruction in an unfathomable way, which we hope, of course, will never be the case."

items.title: NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte says he's had "several conversations this week" with Trump
items.standfirst: After President Trump called NATO a "paper tiger without the U.S.," NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte told "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan" that the alliance has had "several conversations this week" with Mr. Trump and the "good news" is that 22 nations, including many in NATO, have since "come together."
items.content: After President Trump called NATO a "paper tiger without the U.S.," NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte told "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan" that the alliance has had "several conversations this week" with Mr. Trump and the "good news" is that 22 nations, including many in NATO, have since "come together."

items.title: Mike Waltz says "I would never take anything off the table" on bombing Iran nuclear plants
items.standfirst: President Trump has threatened to bomb Iran's nuclear plants and civilian energy infrastructure. U.S. Ambassador to the U.N. Mike Waltz told "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan" that all options remain possible and he would "never take anything off the table for this president."
items.content: President Trump has threatened to bomb Iran's nuclear plants and civilian energy infrastructure. U.S. Ambassador to the U.N. Mike Waltz told "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan" that all options remain possible and he would "never take anything off the table for this president."

bullet_summary: Reported by France24, Guardian World

items.title: Voter turnout reaches 48,1% at 5pm in second round of municipal elections
items.standfirst: Voter turnout climbed to 48,1 percent at 5pm Sunday's second round of council elections, a slight decrease compared to last Sunday's turnout at the same time which reached 48,9 percent. FRANCE 24's Antonia Kerrigan reports from Paris.
items.content: Voter turnout climbed to 48,1 percent at 5pm Sunday's second round of council elections, a slight decrease compared to last Sunday's turnout at the same time which reached 48,9 percent. FRANCE 24's Antonia Kerrigan reports from Paris.

items.title: France votes: turnout sees uptick at midday in major cities
items.standfirst: Following a historically low turnout in the first round of local elections last Sunday, polling stations in major cities, including Paris, are seeing a slight uptick in voter participation around midday compared to 2014 and 2020, FRANCE 24’s Jamie Smith-Maillet reported from Paris.
items.content: Following a historically low turnout in the first round of local elections last Sunday, polling stations in major cities, including Paris, are seeing a slight uptick in voter participation around midday compared to 2014 and 2020, FRANCE 24’s Jamie Smith-Maillet reported from Paris.

items.title: 'Close race': Paris to elect its mayor among three candidates
items.standfirst: French voters returned to the polls Sunday for the second and final round of municipal elections in over 1,500 communes. The race is expected to be extremely tight in Paris with Emmanuel Grégoire, heading a united left and green list, competing with conservative Rachida Dati and hard-left La France Insoumise candidate Sophia Chikirou. FRANCE 24's Antonia Kerrigan reports from Paris.
items.content: French voters returned to the polls Sunday for the second and final round of municipal elections in over 1,500 communes. The race is expected to be extremely tight in Paris with Emmanuel Grégoire, heading a united left and green list, competing with conservative Rachida Dati and hard-left La France Insoumise candidate Sophia Chikirou. FRANCE 24's Antonia Kerrigan reports from Paris.

items.title: French voters head to the polls in second round of local elections
items.standfirst: French voters are heading to the polls in the second round of local elections to elect the mayor of some 1,500 communes, including major cities such as Paris, Lyon and Marseille. FRANCE 24's Antonia Kerrigan reports from Paris.
items.content: French voters are heading to the polls in the second round of local elections to elect the mayor of some 1,500 communes, including major cities such as Paris, Lyon and Marseille. FRANCE 24's Antonia Kerrigan reports from Paris.

items.title: Mayoral races in balance as voting opens in last round of French local elections
items.standfirst: Electoral alliances expected to play vital role in number of contests including Paris and MarseilleVoting is under way in France in the second round of local elections seen as a bellwether for next year’s presidential race – with cities including Paris and Marseille in the balance and both the radical left and far right hoping for gains.Most of France’s 35,000-odd communes elected their councils in the first round last Sunday, but in municipalities where the contest is tighter, including most large urban areas, the second round will be decisive, with electoral alliances playing a key role. Continue reading...

items.title: Iranian missiles hit southern Israel in major escalation of war
items.standfirst: Iranian missiles hit southern Israel overnight near its main nuclear facility at Dimona, injuring at least 170 people according to the authorities and causing heavy destruction. Charlie D'Agata reports from the worst-hit town of Arad.
items.content: Iranian missiles hit southern Israel overnight near its main nuclear facility at Dimona, injuring at least 170 people according to the authorities and causing heavy destruction. Charlie D'Agata reports from the worst-hit town of Arad.

items.title: Nuclear sites targeted across Middle East amid war with Iran
items.standfirst: In an alarming escalation during the U.S. war with Iran, nuclear sites have been targeted after Iran says a uranium enrichment complex was attacked. In response, an Iranian missile struck a town in Israel that’s home to a major nuclear facility, injuring dozens of people. NBC News’ Raf Sanchez reports from Beirut.

clusters.title: Ex-FBI director Robert Mueller, who investigated Russia-Trump campaign ties, dies aged 81
clusters.neutral_headline: Robert Mueller, ex-FBI chief who led Trump-Russia investigation, dies at 81
bullet_summary: Reported by France24, BBC World

items.title: Ex-FBI director Robert Mueller, who investigated Russia-Trump campaign ties, dies aged 81
items.standfirst: Robert S. Mueller III, the FBI director who transformed the nation's premier law enforcement agency into a terrorism-fighting force after the Sept. 11, 2001, attacks and who later became special counsel in charge of investigating ties between Russia and Donald Trump's presidential campaign, has died. He was 81.
items.content: Robert S. Mueller III, the FBI director who transformed the nation's premier law enforcement agency into a terrorism-fighting force after the Sept. 11, 2001, attacks and who later became special counsel in charge of investigating ties between Russia and Donald Trump's presidential campaign, has died. He was 81.

items.standfirst: Trivy vulnerability scanner supply chain compromise Trivy, a widely used vulnerability scanner associated with Aqua Security, was compromised as part of an ongoing supply chain attack. The scope described in the coverage indicates that hackers managed to affect essentially all versions of Trivy by…
items.content: Trivy vulnerability scanner supply chain compromise Trivy, a widely used vulnerability scanner associated with Aqua Security, was compromised as part of an ongoing supply chain attack. The scope described in the coverage indicates that hackers managed to affect essentially all versions of Trivy by…

items.title: Widely used Trivy scanner compromised in ongoing supply-chain attack
items.standfirst: Admins: Sorry to say, but it's likely a rotate-your-secrets kind of weekend.
items.content: Hackers have compromised virtually all versions of Aqua Security’s widely used Trivy vulnerability scanner in an ongoing supply chain attack that could have wide-ranging consequences for developers and the organizations that use them. Trivy maintainer Itay Shakury confirmed the compromise on Friday, following rumors and a thread, since deleted by the attackers, discussing the incident. The attack began in the early hours of Thursday. When it was done, the threat actor had used stolen credentials to force-push all but one of the trivy-action tags and seven setup-trivy tags to use malicious dependencies. Assume your pipelines are compromised A forced push is a git command that overrides a default safety mechanism that protects against overwriting existing commits. Trivy is a vulnerability scanner that developers use to detect vulnerabilities and inadvertently hardcoded authentication secrets in pipelines for developing and deploying software updates. The scanner has 33,200 stars on GitHub, a high rating that indicates it’s used widely.Read full article Comments
